items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: the fundamental attribution error in everyday judgments
items.transcript: Picture this: someone cuts you off in traffic, and your first thought is, what a reckless, selfish driver. But when you cut someone off last week, you knew it was because you were rushing to the hospital. That gap in reasoning is what social psychologists call the fundamental attribution error. The idea is simple but powerful. When we explain other people's behavior, we tend to overemphasize their personality or character, and we underestimate the situation they are in. When we explain our own behavior, though, we do the opposite. We point to the circumstances. A classic study from the sixties showed this clearly. Researchers asked participants to read essays that either supported or opposed a political leader. Even when participants were told the writer had been assigned a position completely at random, with no choice at all, they still assumed the essay reflected the writer's true beliefs. The situation was staring them in the face, and they ignored it. Why does this happen? Part of the reason is attention. When you watch someone act, the person is the most vivid thing in your view, and the situation fades into the background. So the person seems like the cause. This error shapes everyday judgments in ways we rarely notice. We label a quiet coworker as unfriendly rather than shy, or a struggling student as lazy rather than overwhelmed. Recognizing the fundamental attribution error will not erase it, but it can make us pause before we judge. Next time you are quick to blame someone's character, ask yourself what situation they might be facing.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: A common bias in how people explain others' behavior
options.B: A political essay study conducted in the sixties
options.C: How drivers make decisions under time pressure
options.D: The way personality traits develop over a lifetime

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括了整堂课的核心，即人们倾向于用性格而非情境来解释他人行为，这一偏差贯穿全文。B只是课中用来说明的一个实验，属于too_narrow，只抓住一个细节。C借用了开头堵车的例子，但那只是引子，讲座主题不是驾驶决策，属wrong_topic。D使用了lecture中出现的personality一词，但讲座并未讨论性格如何形成，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the essay study find?

options.A: Participants refused to read essays assigned at random
options.B: Readers assumed essays reflected the writer's real views
options.C: Writers changed their beliefs after being assigned a side
options.D: Participants credited the situation over the writer's character

questions.explanation: 正确答案B与讲座一致：即使被告知立场是随机分配的，参与者仍认为文章反映作者真实信念。A改变了关键事实，讲座中参与者阅读了文章而非拒绝，属wrong_detail。C说作者改变了信念，讲座并未提及，属topic_adjacent。D把结论说反了，参与者恰恰忽略了情境、看重性格，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention cutting someone off in traffic?

options.A: To explain why traffic accidents are so common today
options.B: To argue that most drivers are inherently selfish people
options.C: To illustrate how we judge others and ourselves differently
options.D: To describe a driver who was rushing to the hospital

questions.explanation: 正确答案C点明了这个例子的用途：对比我们如何评价他人（怪罪性格）与评价自己（归因情境）。A误认为目的是解释交通事故成因，属wrong_purpose。B把教授没有表达的观点强加给他，教授并不认为司机天生自私，属over_inference。D只是复述例子的字面内容，而非解释为什么要举这个例子，属literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the fundamental attribution error?

options.A: It can be completely eliminated with enough effort
options.B: People blame the situation more than a person's character
options.C: It occurs only when observing strangers, not friends
options.D: Awareness of it may help us judge others less hastily

questions.explanation: 正确答案D符合结尾的暗示：认识到这一偏差虽不能消除它，但能让我们下判断前先停一停。A说可以彻底消除，但教授明确说无法erase，属over_generalization。B把方向说反了，人们评价他人时是看重性格而非情境，属reversed_logic。C限定只对陌生人发生，讲座并未如此限定，属topic_adjacent。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript: When you listen to a song today, you are hearing something that could never happen live. That is not a criticism. It is a defining feature of modern music, and it grew directly out of recording technology. For most of history, a composer wrote music to be performed in a single continuous take, in a room, in real time. The recording changed that assumption completely. Consider the practice of multitracking, developed in the nineteen forties and fifties. Instead of capturing all the musicians at once, engineers could record each part separately and layer them afterward. A singer could harmonize with herself. A guitarist could play parts that no single pair of hands could manage at once. Suddenly the studio itself became an instrument. Composers began to write with editing in mind. They would plan pieces knowing that a flawless live performance was no longer required. Mistakes could be cut, sections could be spliced together, and tempos could be adjusted after the fact. Some artists took this further, building songs that assembled fragments recorded on different days into a seamless whole. The very idea of a musical work shifted, from a score meant to be played, to a recording meant to be assembled. This raises an interesting question about authorship. If a final track is stitched together from dozens of takes and manipulated in the studio, who exactly is the performer? The lines between composing, performing, and engineering began to blur. And that blurring is exactly why so much twentieth-century popular music sounds the way it does. It was conceived, from the start, as a recording rather than a performance.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The invention of multitracking in the nineteen forties
options.B: How recording reshaped the way music was composed
options.C: Why live performances sound better than recordings
options.D: The training required to become a studio engineer

questions.explanation: 正确答案B抓住主旨：录音技术如何改变了作曲的方式。A只是讲座中的一个例子，多轨录音的发明，属too_narrow。C讨论现场与录音孰优孰劣，讲座并未做此比较，属wrong_topic。D借用了engineer一词，但讲座不讲工程师培训，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did multitracking allow?

options.A: Musicians to perform an entire piece in one continuous take
options.B: Audiences to record concerts from their own seats
options.C: Each part to be recorded separately and layered later
options.D: Engineers to capture all the musicians simultaneously

questions.explanation: 正确答案C与讲座一致：多轨录音让各声部分别录制再叠加。A说一次连续录完，那是旧做法，与多轨相反，属wrong_detail。B说观众自己录音，讲座未提，属topic_adjacent。D说同时录下所有乐手，这正是多轨要取代的旧方式，方向说反，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor ask who the performer is?

options.A: To criticize engineers for taking credit from musicians
options.B: To claim that studio recordings have no real authorship
options.C: To note that a final track can combine dozens of takes
options.D: To highlight how recording blurred traditional musical roles

questions.explanation: 正确答案D点明这个反问的目的：说明录音使作曲、演奏、工程之间的界限变得模糊。A把目的说成批评工程师抢功，讲座并无此意，属wrong_purpose。B断言录音毫无作者身份，是把观点推得过头，属over_inference。C只是复述录音由多条take拼成的字面事实，而非解释提问的用意，属literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about much twentieth-century popular music?

options.A: It was designed as a recording, not a live performance
options.B: It can never be performed by live musicians at all
options.C: It aimed to reproduce a single continuous take faithfully
options.D: It relied mainly on advances in concert-hall acoustics

questions.explanation: 正确答案A符合结尾暗示：这些音乐从一开始就是作为录音而非现场演出来构思的。B说它根本无法现场演奏，把特定观点推向绝对，属over_generalization。C说它力求忠实再现一次连续录音，与全文相反，属reversed_logic。D把原因归于音乐厅声学的进步，讲座讲的是录音技术，属topic_adjacent。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript: Before the middle of the nineteenth century, most serious painting was made inside a studio. An artist might sketch a landscape outdoors, then return indoors to compose the actual painting from memory and imagination. The Impressionists broke with that habit, and the reason was surprisingly practical. In the eighteen forties, paint began to be sold in small, sealed metal tubes. Before that, painters ground their own pigments and mixed them with oil, a messy process that was nearly impossible to manage out in a field. The tube made paint portable. For the first time, an artist could carry a full palette outside and paint a scene directly in front of it, in natural light. Painting outdoors, the Impressionists noticed something that studio work had hidden. Sunlight is not constant. A haystack at dawn is not the color it is at noon, and a shadow is rarely just gray or black. It carries hints of blue and violet reflected from the sky. So they began placing small strokes of pure, unmixed color side by side, letting the viewer's eye blend them at a distance. Rather than mixing a dull gray on the palette, they might set a stroke of blue right beside a stroke of orange. This approach transformed how color was understood. Color was no longer a fixed property of an object, but something that shifted moment to moment with the light. What looks like a loose, spontaneous style was actually built on careful observation of how light and color really behave. And it all started, in part, with something as simple as a metal tube.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How metal paint tubes were manufactured in the eighteen forties
options.B: The techniques used to grind raw pigments in a studio
options.C: Why Impressionists painted outdoors and how it changed color
options.D: The role of memory and imagination in landscape art

questions.explanation: 正确答案C概括主旨：印象派为何到户外作画，以及这如何改变了对色彩的理解。A只关注颜料管这一细节，且讲座并未讲其制造，属too_narrow。B说研磨颜料的技术，那只是被取代的旧做法，属wrong_topic。D使用了讲座出现的memory和imagination字眼，但那描述的是旧的室内作画方式，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what made painting outdoors practical?

options.A: Artists learned to grind their pigments more quickly in the field
options.B: Studios installed large windows to let in more natural light
options.C: Painters memorized scenes and completed them later indoors
options.D: Paint became portable once it was sold in sealed metal tubes

questions.explanation: 正确答案D与讲座一致：颜料装入密封金属管后变得便携，才使户外作画成为可能。A说在野外更快研磨颜料，讲座说研磨在野外几乎无法进行，属wrong_detail。B说画室装大窗，讲座未提，属topic_adjacent。C说凭记忆回室内完成，那正是被印象派抛弃的旧方式，方向相反，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention a haystack at dawn and at noon?

options.A: To show that sunlight changes an object's apparent color
options.B: To explain why haystacks were a favorite rural subject
options.C: To argue that studio painting was completely worthless
options.D: To note that shadows can contain blue and violet

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明这个例子的用途：说明阳光会改变物体呈现的颜色。B误认为目的是解释为何常画干草堆，属wrong_purpose。C把教授的意思推向极端，他从未说室内绘画毫无价值，属over_inference。D只是复述阴影含蓝紫色的字面事实，而非解释干草堆例子的用意，属literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the Impressionists' loose style?

options.A: It required no planning or genuine skill of any kind
options.B: It rested on careful observation of light and color
options.C: It treated color as a fixed property of each object
options.D: It was mainly a reaction against the high cost of pigments

questions.explanation: 正确答案B符合结尾暗示：看似随意的风格实则建立在对光与色的细致观察之上。A说毫无计划与技巧，把观点推得过头，属over_generalization。C说颜色是物体固定属性，与讲座相反，讲座强调颜色随光变化，属reversed_logic。D把动因归为对颜料价格的反应，讲座未如此说，属topic_adjacent。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: the concept of ecological succession after a wildfire
items.transcript: A forest fire looks like an ending. The trees are blackened, the ground is ash, and the animals are gone. But to an ecologist, a burned landscape is really a beginning. It is the opening chapter of a process called ecological succession. Succession is the sequence of changes a community goes through as it rebuilds after a disturbance. After a fire, the first species to return are what we call pioneer species. These are typically fast-growing plants, grasses, wildflowers, and certain shrubs, whose seeds spread easily and thrive in open, sunlit soil. Some plants are even specially adapted to fire. Certain pine cones, for instance, are sealed shut with resin and only pop open to release their seeds when they are heated by flames. Think about that for a moment. For these species, a fire is not a catastrophe. It is a trigger for reproduction. As the pioneer plants grow, they change the environment around them. They add organic matter to the soil, provide shade, and help retain moisture. These changes make the site hospitable for slower-growing species, first shrubs, then small trees, then the tall trees that eventually form a mature forest. Each stage prepares the ground for the next. This is why ecologists no longer see fire as purely destructive. Many ecosystems actually depend on periodic fires to clear out old growth and reset the cycle. Suppressing every fire, it turns out, can allow dead material to build up and lead to far more severe blazes later. What looks like destruction is often just one turn in a long, renewing cycle.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How certain pine cones release their seeds when heated
options.B: The methods firefighters use to suppress large wildfires
options.C: Why forest animals flee from blackened landscapes
options.D: How ecosystems rebuild through succession after a fire

questions.explanation: 正确答案D概括主旨：生态系统在火灾后如何通过演替逐步重建。A只讲松果放种这一细节，属too_narrow。B说消防员灭火的方法，讲座并不讨论，属wrong_topic。C借用了开头blackened landscape的字眼，但主题不是动物逃离，属surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention pine cones sealed with resin?

options.A: To show that some species are adapted to depend on fire
options.B: To explain how most forest fires typically get started
options.C: To argue that every plant species benefits from wildfires
options.D: To note that the cones open only when heated by flames

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明例子的用途：说明有些物种已适应甚至依赖火来繁殖。B误以为目的是解释火灾如何引发，属wrong_purpose。C把观点推向绝对，说所有植物都受益于火，讲座并未如此断言，属over_inference。D只是复述松果遇热才开的字面事实，而非解释举例的用意，属literal_restatement。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what do pioneer species do to the environment?

options.A: They release resin that seals off the surrounding soil
options.B: They add organic matter, provide shade, and retain moisture
options.C: They compete with tall trees for sunlight and water
options.D: They dry out the soil and block later plant growth

questions.explanation: 正确答案B与讲座一致：先锋植物为土壤增加有机质、提供遮阴并保持水分。A把松果的resin安到先锋植物身上，改变了事实，属wrong_detail。C说它们与高大乔木争夺光水，讲座未提这种竞争，属topic_adjacent。D说它们使土壤干燥、阻碍后续生长，与讲座正相反，属reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about suppressing every fire?

options.A: Fires should never be controlled under any circumstances
options.B: Preventing fires keeps ecosystems healthier over time
options.C: It can ultimately make future fires more severe
options.D: It mainly threatens the survival of large forest animals

questions.explanation: 正确答案C符合结尾暗示：压制每一场火会让枯枝落叶堆积，导致日后更严重的火灾。A说火永远不该被控制，把观点推向绝对，属over_generalization。B说防火使生态系统更健康，与讲座相反，属reversed_logic。D说主要威胁大型动物，讲座未提及此，属topic_adjacent。
